title: Writing Your First Test
intro: You've decided to add tests to your task manager. You open the testing framework docs and stare at the blank test file. It's like writing a lab report in science class — set up the experiment, run it, and check the results. Let's walk through writing your first real test.

teach.title: Anatomy of a Test
teach.body: Every test has three phases: Arrange (set up the data), Act (run the function), and Assert (check the result). A test for adding a task would create input data, call the addTask function, and verify the returned task has the right properties.

teach.title: Writing a Test Is Like Writing a Lab Report
teach.body: In science class, you set up an experiment (arrange), run it (act), and check the result against your hypothesis (assert). Writing a test follows the exact same steps — you're just the scientist and your code is the experiment.

teach.title: A Simple Unit Test
teach.annotation: The Arrange-Act-Assert pattern keeps tests readable and focused on one behavior.

teach.body: Writing your first test is a milestone — it proves you can build software professionally. Apps with tests attract paying users because they work reliably, and that reliability is what turns a side project into income.
Start with the simplest test possible — one function, one input, one expected output. Build complexity gradually. A test that exists and runs is better than a perfect test you never write.

start.narrative: You're writing your first test for the task manager's 'completeTask' function. It should mark a task as done and set a completedAt timestamp. Where do you start?

choices.text: Write a test that checks the entire task lifecycle — create, complete, delete — all in one test
Write a focused test: given a pending task, completeTask should set status to 'done' and add a completedAt date
Skip writing tests and just manually click through the app to check it works

too-much.narrative: Your test is 40 lines long, creates a task, completes it, verifies it, then deletes it. When it fails, you can't tell which part broke. You split it into three separate tests, each checking one behavior. Now you need to set up the inputs each one needs.

choices.text: Define a fresh pending task object inside each test — clear, no shared state
Reuse one task object across all three tests so the setup isn't repeated

focused-test.narrative: You scoped the test to one behavior. Now you need to set up the input data — a pending task that completeTask can act on. How do you arrange it?

choices.text: Use a tiny factory: makeTask({ status: 'pending' }) — readable, defaults filled in
Build the task object inline with every required field spelled out
Insert a real task into the database before the test runs

skip-tests.narrative: You manually test and it seems to work. Two weeks later, you refactor the task model and the completeTask function silently breaks — it no longer sets the timestamp. Users complain their completed tasks show no completion date. You finally agree to write a test.

choices.text: Start with a focused test for the regression: a pending task in, a completed task with timestamp out
Just patch the bug and ship — write tests later when you have more time

act-assert.narrative: The arrange step is ready. Now you call completeTask(task). What do you assert about the result?

choices.text: Assert exactly the two contracts: status === 'done' AND completedAt is a Date — one test, two clear expectations
Snapshot the whole returned object and let the framework diff it on future runs
Just assert the result is truthy — if it returned anything, it probably worked

edge-cases.narrative: The happy-path test passes. Bugs hide in edge cases. Which edge case do you cover next?

choices.text: Calling completeTask on an already-completed task — should it throw, no-op, or overwrite the timestamp?
Passing null as the task argument — should fail with a clear error
Skip edge cases — the happy path is enough for a first test

ci-runs.narrative: Two tests in, both passing locally. Where should they run?

choices.text: On every pull request — failing tests block merges, so no regression makes it to main
Only as a pre-commit hook on your machine — fast feedback for you, no CI cost
Run them by hand when you remember — automation can come later

excellent-end.narrative: Your first test stack is small but real: a focused happy-path test, a deliberate edge-case test, both gated on every PR. When the next refactor accidentally touches completeTask, the failed CI check fires before the bug ever reaches a user.
excellent-end.feedback: This is the loop testing was invented for: write the smallest test that proves a behavior, add an edge case, automate so a regression cannot ship. You skipped the trap of either over-testing (mega-tests) or under-testing (none at all).

mediocre-end.narrative: Your tests run on your machine, but never on a teammate's PR. Two weeks later, someone pushes a change you didn't review and the regression slips through.
mediocre-end.feedback: Local hooks help the author, but tests pay the most when they protect the whole team. Move them to CI so every PR — yours or not — is gated.

weak-end.narrative: You wrote the tests, then forgot about them. Three months later they don't even compile because the function signature changed.
weak-end.feedback: A test that never runs is a test that doesn't exist. Pick one place — pre-commit, CI, or both — and wire it up the day you write the test, not later.
